ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for canned raspberry in syrup; the broader canned fruit food product class (FOODON_03301710) is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents canned raspberries; the syrup variant is captured via the processing descriptor. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for canned raspberry in syrup as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned raspberry in syrup is approximately 125 g (about half a cup of drained fruit with a small amount of syrup), commonly used as a dessert topping, yogurt accompaniment, or standalone snack.

nutrient_source.title: Canned Raspberry in Syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030016 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible for fruit in syrup per USDA FoodData Central cross-reference, NDB 09431 — Raspberries, canned, red, heavy syrup pack, solids and liquids). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canned raspberry in heavy syrup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: canned fruit in heavy syrup (peaches, pears) which cluster at GI 38–43 in the Sydney GI Database, and the known low GI of raspberries (~25 fresh). The added sugar syrup raises the effective GI above fresh raspberry but remains in the low category. Glycemic load per 125 g serving = (40 × 17.0 × 1.25) / 100 = 8.5 g, classified as low GL. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement if one becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (16.8 g/100 g) from added syrup; sodium is very low at 5 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is negligible (5 mg/100 g), the high added sugar load (16.8 g/100 g) can promote insulin-mediated sodium retention and contribute to elevated blood pressure; individuals with hypertension should prefer fresh or frozen raspberries (~4.4 g sugar/100 g) and, if consuming canned, drain the syrup thoroughly and limit portions to 80–100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 16.8 g/100 g from added syrup; estimated GI 40 (low category) but glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high relative to the sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high added sugar content (16.8 g/100 g) significantly elevates glycaemic load compared with fresh raspberries; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit canned raspberries in syrup, preferring fresh or frozen raspberries (GI ~25, sugar ~4.4 g/100 g, fibre 6.5 g/100 g) which are well-suited for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (16.8 g/100 g) raises triglyceride risk; fat is negligible (0.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol is zero
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat and cholesterol profile is favourable (fat 0.1 g/100 g, cholesterol 0 mg/100 g), but the high added sugar from syrup (16.8 g/100 g) can elevate serum triglycerides and contribute to cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit intake and choose fresh or frozen raspberries to benefit from anthocyanins and polyphenols without the added sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (16.8 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory, negating the anti-inflammatory anthocyanin benefits of raspberries
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added syrup sugar (16.8 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory mediators and overrides the natural anti-inflammatory benefits of raspberry anthocyanins and ellagic acid; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose fresh or frozen raspberries to access these benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned raspberry in syrup is a processed fruit product consisting of whole or halved red raspberries preserved in a sugar-based heavy syrup, typically packed in cans or glass jars. The canning process involves heating the fruit in syrup to achieve shelf stability, which softens the berries and concentrates their flavour while the syrup adds significant sweetness. The resulting product has a deep red colour, a soft and slightly mushy texture compared to fresh raspberries, and a sweet-tart flavour profile dominated by the added sugar. Per 100 g, the product provides 75 kcal, 0.6 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 17.0 g carbohydrate (of which 16.8 g is sugar), 2.5 g dietary fibre, and only 5 mg sodium. Potassium is 78 mg/100 g and iron is 0.95 mg/100 g. The high sugar content (16.8 g/100 g) from the added syrup is the dominant nutritional concern, making this product unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. The estimated glycemic index is 40 (low category), but the glycemic load per 125 g serving is approximately 8.5 g, which is clinically meaningful for diabetic meal planning. Fresh or frozen raspberries are strongly preferred for clinical populations due to their much lower sugar content (~4.4 g/100 g), higher fibre (6.5 g/100 g), and preserved anthocyanin and polyphenol content. Canned raspberries in syrup are commonly used as a dessert topping, pastry filling, ice cream accompaniment, or standalone snack, and are widely available in Western and international markets.

notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F04030016, and detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data for this specific canned product are not available in the HPB database as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09431 — Raspberries, canned, red, heavy syrup pack) was consulted as a cross-reference for saturated fat (confirmed 0.0 g/100 g, negligible). 2. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for canned raspberry in syrup per USDA FoodData Central NDB 09431 cross-reference; this is a fruit product with no added fat. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a plant-based fruit product containing no animal-derived ingredients. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on canned fruit in heavy syrup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for canned raspberry in syrup was found. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific product. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules.
